_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). General Medical: Hypoglycemia. NASEMSO national provisional base.
level_display: All Levels — Interventions Scoped Per Provider Level
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — General Medical: Hypoglycemia

sections.title: Patient Care Goals
sections.reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — General Medical: Hypoglycemia
points: Limit morbidity from hypoglycemia by: a. Describing appropriate use of glucose monitoring
b. Treating symptomatic hypoglycemia

sections.title: Patient Presentation
points: Patients with blood glucose less than 60 mg/dL with symptoms of hypoglycemia
Patients with altered level of consciousness [See Altered Mental Status Guideline]
Patients with stroke symptoms (e.g., hemiparesis, dysarthria) [See Suspected Stroke/Transient Ischemic Attack Guideline]
Patients with seizure [See Seizures Guideline]
Patients with history of diabetes and other medical symptoms
Patients with suspected alcohol ingestion
Patients with metabolic disorders (glycogen storage disease, fatty oxidation or organic acid disorders, maple syrup urine disease)
Patients who appear to be intoxicated
EXCLUSION CRITERIA:
Patient in cardiac arrest
Patient with normal mental status in absence of inclusion criteria listed above

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: Oral glucose 15-25 g for alert patient who can swallow
Safety: protect unconscious patient from aspiration — never give oral glucose to unconscious patient
Oxygen via NRM if altered mental status or respiratory distress
Lateral recumbent position (recovery position) if unconscious

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: Oral glucose (15-25 g) for any patient who can swallow and cooperate
Reassess blood glucose in 15 minutes — repeat dose if BGL <60 mg/dL and patient able to swallow
12-lead ECG acquisition if dysrhythmia suspected
IV maintenance (non-medicated)

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: IV/IO access initiation
D10W 250 mL IV/IO (preferred — less tissue damage than D50W if extravasation)
D50W 25 g (50 mL) IV/IO for rapid correction in unconscious patient
Glucagon 1 mg IM/SQ for hypoglycemia if IV access unavailable
Reassess BGL 10-15 minutes after treatment; repeat if BGL still <70 mg/dL
Waveform capnography if airway concern

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: D50W 25-50 g IV/IO if patient unconscious and IV available (preferred over D10W for speed)
Glucagon 1-2 mg IV/IO for severe hypoglycemia when IV access unavailable
Thiamine 100 mg IV before dextrose in suspected alcohol-related or malnourished patients
Investigate and treat underlying cause: insulin overdose, oral hypoglycemics, adrenal insufficiency
12-lead ECG interpretation for hypoglycemia-induced dysrhythmia
For sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia: octreotide 50 mcg SQ/IV to prevent recurrence (per protocol)

sections.title: Patient Safety Considerations
points: Dextrose 10% can be safely used in all ages of patient. Dextrose 10% works as effectively and quickly as other concentrations

Dextrose 50% can cause local tissue damage if it extravasates from vein and may cause hyperglycemia. Dextrose 50% carries risk for little clinical gain. EMS systems may consider carrying no more than 25% concentration of dextrose for treating hypoglycemia in adults
For children less than 8 years old, dextrose concentration of no more than 25% should be used
For neonates and infants less than 1 month of age, dextrose concentration of no more than 10'12.5% should be used

Sulfonylureas (e.g., glyburide, glipizide) have long half-lives ranging from 12'60 hrs. Patients with corrected hypoglycemia who are taking these agents are at particular risk for recurrent symptoms and frequently require hospital admission 1. Using 10% dextrose is as effective and safer than other stronger concentrations
Consider contribution of oral diabetic medications to hypoglycemia

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: Concomitant trauma
Diaphoresis or hypothermia may be associated with hypoglycemia

sections.title: Key Documentation
points: ' Document reassessment of vital signs and mental status after administration of glucose/dextrose/glucagon
' Document point of care glucose level (if in scope of practice) when indicated

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: Contact medical direction for clinical deterioration or interventions outside standard scope
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
